stories.title: Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky
stories.excerpt: Colliers completed the sale of Gray Industrial Center Building 2, a newly completed 302,400-square-foot industrial facility at 530 Logistics Drive in Shelbyville, Kentucky, approximately 30 miles east of Louisville. The build-to-suit distribution facility is fully leased to Kuehne+Nagel, a global logistics provider. Colliers Industrial Capital Markets advisors Alex Cantu, Alex Davenport, Jeff Devine, Steve Disse and Tyler Ziebel, together with local CBRE leasing advisors Doug Butcher and Alex Grove, represented Gray Development. The property was sold to ElmTree Funds in a forward-funded acquisition that closed upon completion of the facility. “Gray Industrial Center Building 2 demonstrates the continued demand for build-to-suit industrial facilities in strategically located markets like Shelbyville, where access to the I-64 corridor and the greater Louisville region continues to attract both occupiers and institutional capital,” said Alex Cantu. “Forward-funded acquisitions of newly completed, fully leased assets remain highly attractive to investors seeking modern industrial product supported by long-term tenancy and strong market fundamentals.” Situated on approximately 32.19 acres, the Gray Industrial Center Building is located approximately one mile from Interstate 64. The property provides convenient access to Louisville International Airport and UPS Worldport. The post Colliers Arranges Sale of 302K-SF Industrial Building in Kentucky appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kuehne+Nagel leased 302K SF build-to-suit in Shelbyville, KY; ElmTree Funds acquired completed asset.
Property 30 miles east of Louisville near I-64, UPS Worldport; forward-funded institutional acquisition.
Demonstrates continued demand for logistics real estate in strategically positioned secondary markets.
stories.summary: Colliers arranged the sale of Gray Industrial Center Building 2, a fully leased 302,400-square-foot distribution facility in Shelbyville, Kentucky (30 miles east of Louisville), to institutional investor ElmTree Funds upon completion. The property, leased to global logistics provider Kuehne+Nagel, benefited from proximity to I-64 and UPS Worldport. Forward-funded acquisitions of fully leased, newly completed industrial assets remain attractive to investors seeking stable, modern product.
stories.impact: Build-to-suit logistics real estate velocity in secondary markets near major distribution hubs (Louisville/I-64 corridor) signals sustained e-commerce and supply-chain demand; institutional capital appetite for stabilized, long-term triple-net deals remains strong, supporting development feasibility in regional logistics clusters. Louisville area presents underlying tailwinds for multifamily if logistics job growth follows.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced the sale of a net-leased restaurant property occupied by Storming Crab in Rockford, Illinois.“This offering generated significant investor interest due to the property’s strong operating performance, prime location and the limited supply of comparable retail assets in the market,” said Brian Parmacek, managing director of investments. “The asset received multiple offers above the asking price, ultimately attracting a private investor focused on securing long-term cash flow and income generation.”Parmacek, investment specialist in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Downtown office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, RCJD Canyon Crest, LLC, and procured the buyer, MBOGO Properties, LLC. The 5,860-square-foot freestanding property is located at 6779 E. State St. and is occupied by Storming Crab, a chain of casual restaurants specializing in Cajun-style seafood boils. The restaurant is surrounded by national retailers, including Target, ALDI, Ulta Beauty, Hobby Lobby, Burlington and PetSmart and is less than a half-mile from Hard Rock Casino Rockford. The post Marcus & Millichap Brokers Sale of Net-Leased Storming Crab Restaurant in Rockford appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Net-leased Storming Crab restaurant in Rockford, IL sold above asking; multiple offers received.
Property near Hard Rock Casino Rockford, surrounded by Target, ALDI, Ulta, national retailers.
Limited supply of comparable retail assets in Rockford attracted private income-focused buyer.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of a 5,860-square-foot net-leased Storming Crab restaurant property in Rockford, Illinois, which attracted multiple offers above asking and closed to a private investor seeking long-term cash flow. The freestanding building is located at 6779 E. State Street, surrounded by national retailers and within a half-mile of Hard Rock Casino Rockford. Strong investor demand reflected both the asset's operating performance and the scarcity of comparable retail properties in the market.
stories.impact: Limited retail supply in Rockford despite anchor tenants and casino proximity suggests underlying demand density; local demographic strength may support multifamily development if consumer spending and retail anchoring signal population retention and wage growth.

stories.title: Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap finalized the sale of 312 Bowery, a multifamily property in Manhattan’s NoHo neighborhood, for $16.65 million. John Stewart and Dylan Torey of Marcus & Millichap exclusively marketed the property on behalf of the seller, Bremen House, Inc., and procured the buyer, Allied Realty & Development Corp. “Buyer demand was some of the strongest we’ve seen this year due to the scarcity of truly free-market multifamily properties,” said Stewart. “Well-maintained properties in core locations, with no historical rent regulation or with attorney-approved deregulation documentation, will continue to attract the most engaged buyers, with bidding wars and, in some cases, sub-five-cap final pricing.” The five-story, 23,000-square-foot building in the NoHo East historic district was built in 1920 and converted to multifamily in 2004. It includes 22 modernized free-market apartments. The property has approximately 7,000 square feet of unused development rights and a 3,900-square-foot ground-floor commercial space occupied by The Hole, an art gallery. The post Free-Market NoHo Apartments Generate Strong Buyer Demand appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Free-market NYC multifamily commands sub-5% cap rates; 22-unit NoHo building sold for $16.65M.
Deregulation and absence of rent control drive buyer competition; bidding wars common.
Building has 7,000 SF unused development rights; historic district conversion adds value.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap facilitated the sale of 312 Bowery, a 22-unit free-market apartment building in Manhattan's NoHo neighborhood, for $16.65 million, generating "some of the strongest buyer demand this year." The 1920-built, 23,000-square-foot property benefits from deregulation documentation and includes 7,000 square feet of unused development rights plus ground-floor commercial space. Buyers are specifically targeting well-maintained, deregulated Manhattan multifamily assets, with pricing reaching sub-five-cap levels.
stories.impact: NYC free-market multifamily scarcity and sub-5% cap rates signal compressed valuations and limited development windows; deregulation status and development rights are now primary value drivers. Developers seeking value-add or new supply in Manhattan face existential supply constraints—regulatory clarity and deregulation pathways are becoming deal gatekeepers.

stories.title: Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison
stories.excerpt: Nicholas & Associates, the construction management and general contracting arm of the Nicholas Family of Companies, has been selected by Moyer Properties as the general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury rental community set on a 4.86-acre site in Madison, Wisconsin. The ground-up development is the latest in Nicholas & Associates’ Wisconsin portfolio, further expanding the firm’s presence in the state and region. Designed by HKM Architects + Planners, Inc., Aster Flats will offer studio, one-, two- and three-bedroom units ranging in size from 588 to 1,395 square feet. “Being selected to build Aster Flats reflects the trust and strong reputation we’ve established in southeast Wisconsin through a series of high-profile projects in recent years,” said Nick Papnicholas Jr., chief executive officer and managing director of Nicholas Family of Companies. Nicholas & Associates will break ground in August on the new development, with construction happening in phases across the three buildings. Completion is anticipated in February 2028. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Nicholas & Associates Tapped as Contractor for Ground-Up Multifamily Development in Madison appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nicholas & Associates selected as GC for 263-unit Aster Flats in Madison, WI; August 2026 groundbreak.
Three-building luxury community; 4.86-acre site; completion February 2028.
Studio to 3-bed units (588–1,395 SF); phased construction across buildings.
stories.summary: Moyer Properties engaged Nicholas & Associates as general contractor for Aster Flats, a 263-unit, three-building luxury apartment development on 4.86 acres in Madison, Wisconsin. Designed by HKM Architects + Planners, the project breaks ground in August 2026 and will complete in February 2028, built in phases. Units range from studio to three-bedroom (588–1,395 square feet) across the mixed-density site.
stories.impact: Madison multifamily pipeline growth signals developer confidence in market fundamentals; Nicholas & Associates' expanding Wisconsin portfolio suggests strong regional construction cost predictability and labor availability. No supply or cost headwinds indicated; project pacing suggests normal financing and entitlement conditions in Madison market.

stories.title: Largely Vacant Santa Monica Offices Get New Life as School Campus
stories.excerpt: Colliers arranged the all-cash sale of Ocean Park Plaza, a two-story, 100,700-square-foot office and medical building at 2701 Ocean Park Blvd. in Santa Monica. The buyer, an undisclosed co-educational school, plans to relocate to the property and establish its new campus there. Vice chair Sean Fulp, EVPs Mark Schuessler and Todd Tydlaska and associate VPs Jordan Garcia and Blake Hammerstein of Colliers marketed the property and facilitated the transaction on behalf of the seller. Tim Dornin and Scott Rigsby of Industry Partners and Dan Pickart of Newmark represented the buyer. The sale repositions a, largely vacant office building for active community use. Ocean Park Plaza had approximately 73% of its space available for immediate occupancy. The acquisition returns the asset to full utility while removing it from the area’s office inventory. “This is a great outcome for Santa Monica,” said Fulp. “The buyer gets a campus in the heart of the Westside, and the neighborhood gains an institution that adds real activity and stability.” The post Largely Vacant Santa Monica Offices Get New Life as School Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ocean Park Plaza Santa Monica: 100.7K SF office building, 73% vacant; sold to school for campus.
All-cash transaction removes 100K SF from Santa Monica office supply.
Office-to-institutional conversion trend; Santa Monica office market faces persistent structural headwinds.
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the all-cash sale of Ocean Park Plaza, a 100,700-square-foot office and medical building in Santa Monica, to an undisclosed school operator establishing a new campus. The property was 73% vacant at sale. The transaction repositions the asset from struggling office to active institutional use and removes the building from Santa Monica's office inventory.
stories.impact: Conversion of vacant Santa Monica office to institutional (non-residential) use signals permanent office oversupply in Westside LA and structural demand loss; this caps residential-friendly land recycling (office rarely converts to multifamily in Santa Monica's strict zoning). Developer interest should focus on organic multifamily development sites or office-to-residential adaptive-reuse opportunities in more permissive submarkets; Westside office decline does not translate to multifamily tailwinds.

stories.title: Apartment Complex Sells for $51M in Los Angeles’ Miracle Mile
stories.excerpt: Prime Residential acquired the 132-unit complex in the Miracle Mile neighborhood for $51.3 million. An LLC tied to Studio City-based Harrison Properties sold the property at 740 South Burnside Avenue, named Palm Court Apartments, for approximately $388,000 per unit. Marcus & Millichap’s Jeff Louks, Gayle Factor and Elliot Sabag represented the seller, while Matt Ziegler procured the buyer. Ziegler said the seller recently completed extensive upgrades to the property. Palm Court is near Museum Row on Wilshire Boulevard, as well as the Grove shopping complex, the Original Farmers Market, and Metro’s new station at Wilshire and Fairfax Avenue. It was completed in 1988 with 48 one-bedroom apartments, 72 two-bedrooms and 12 three-bedrooms. It also features a pool and spa, a fitness center, saunas and a rooftop sundeck. The acquisition comes as multifamily investment activity picks up in L.A. Investment sales increased roughly 25 percent during the 12-month period ending in March, with L.A. accounting for more than 20 percent of all apartment trades across primary U.S. markets, according to Marcus & Millichap’s most recent market report. However, the city’s Measure ULA transfer tax continues to weigh on larger deals, as transactions above $10 million during the same period were down 50 percent compared to the year before the measure took effect in 2023. L.A.’s multifamily vacancy rate held at 4.8 percent at the start of 2026, while average effective rents increased 1.4 percent since the same period in 2025 to $2,853 per month. Marcus & Millichap forecasts vacancy will reach 5 percent by the end of this year as 8,500 units are added to the market. Prime Residential has owned the landmark Park La Brea community just north of Miracle Mile since 1995. It’s said to be the largest apartment complex west of the Mississippi River. It includes 4,249 rent-controlled units in 18 high-rise towers and 175 garden-style buildings on 144 acres. Gregory Cornfield can be reache
bullets: Prime Residential acquired 132-unit Palm Court for $51.3M; $388K/unit in Miracle Mile, LA.
Recently upgraded property near Museum Row, Grove, Metro Wilshire/Fairfax station.
LA multifamily sales up 25% YoY (12m ending March); LA accounts for 20%+ of US trades.
stories.summary: Prime Residential purchased Palm Court Apartments, a 132-unit complex in Los Angeles' Miracle Mile neighborhood, for $51.3 million ($388,000 per unit). Built in 1988 with recent upgrades, the property benefits from proximity to Museum Row, The Grove shopping center, and Metro's new Wilshire/Fairfax station. LA multifamily investment activity has surged 25% in the past 12 months, with LA now representing over 20% of all apartment trades in primary U.S. markets.
stories.impact: LA multifamily sales velocity (25% YoY growth; 20%+ of national volume) indicates capital concentration and strong buyer activity in primary market; transit access (Metro Wilshire/Fairfax) is now a pricing premium. Developers should prioritize transit-adjacent infill in LA and secondary LA submarkets before land/cap-rate compression accelerates further; competition for Miracle Mile-class assets is intense and rising.

stories.title: Columbia University Acquires Student Housing in Morningside Heights for $122M
stories.excerpt: Columbia University is adding a new student housing project to its dormitory portfolio in northern Manhattan. Sam Charney’s Charney Companies sold its more than 300-unit project at 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights to the Ivy League university for $122 million, a source close to the deal confirmed. The Real Deal first reported the deal. Charney, along with Criterion Real Estate Capital, acquired the 80,000-square-foot building, which was originally a smaller 82-unit dorm next to Riverside Church, from an affiliate of the church for $38 million in 2024, with plans to convert it into luxury student housing. The joint venture secured $55 million of acquisition financing from Madison Realty Capital to purchase and redevelop the historic property, then known as McGiffert Hall, as Commercial Observer previously reported. “The opportunity to restore a historic, irreplaceable 92-year-old building with all the beauty that 99 Claremont offers while providing critically needed best-in-class student housing to New York City is a tremendous one,” Sam Charney said in 2024 following the acquisition. The finished project will be Columbia’s first residence hall dedicated to students in its School of General Studies (GS), according to a press release from Columbia in March. The dormitory will open in the fall. “This milestone reflects our commitment to meeting one of the most significant needs within our student population: accessible, community-centered housing designed specifically for nontraditional undergraduate students,” Lisa Rosen-Metsch, dean of Columbia’s School of General Studies, wrote in the March release. “Its close proximity to Morningside campus will reduce commute times and increase opportunities for students to deepen their engagement with one another, essential for the community building that is a hallmark of the GS experience.” Spokespeople for Charney and Columbia did not immediately respond to requests for comment. In addition to housing more than 300 st
bullets: Columbia University acquired 300+ unit student housing at 99 Claremont Avenue for $122M.
Charney Companies and Criterion purchased building for $38M in 2024; $55M financing; converted from 82-unit dorm.
Institutional buyer removes development asset from market; luxury student housing demand strong.
stories.summary: Columbia University acquired 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights from Charney Companies and Criterion Real Estate Capital for $122 million. Charney and Criterion had purchased the 80,000-square-foot historic property (formerly McGiffert Hall) from an affiliated church for $38 million in 2024, secured $55 million in acquisition financing from Madison Realty Capital, and planned a conversion to 300+ units of luxury student housing. The 92-year-old building sale to the university concludes the development without a third-party outcome.
stories.impact: Institutional buyer (university) acquisition of development-stage student housing removes asset from speculative pipeline; demonstrates strong institutional demand for student housing but signals lower returns for third-party developers at scale. Columbia's direct acquisition strategy suggests housing scarcity and high land value near Ivy League campus—a competitive pressure for independent developers; similar institutional consolidation may spread to other university markets.

stories.title: CRE Comes Together at MADE Bush Terminal for Cocktails and an Exciting New Development
stories.excerpt: On a humid and rainy July night in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, people from throughout the commercial real estate industry escaped the elements at the new mixed-use complex MADE Bush Terminal, which hosted Commercial Observer’s annual summer networking mixer. Amid a series of massive square wooden columns conveying the complex’s industrial feel, attendees networked, enjoyed drinks and small bites, and heard from some of MADE’s pioneering tenants on the benefits of taking space at the complex, which, in addition to sites like a brewery and a new 1,000-capacity music venue, will mostly host companies with light industrial uses. Bridget Chansakul, assistant vice president at the New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC), handles leasing for the development, and was excited for people to see the goings-on at Building A, where the event took place. While several tenants are already in place in that building, as well as in the project’s Building B, the full development and unveiling of MADE Bush Terminal, which will eventually be a five-building complex, will occur slowly over the next few years. “We’re giving a sneak peek tonight, but it’s just the beginning of what’s happening here in Sunset Park,” said Chansakul. “We’re getting ready to announce a new brewery coming to the site and we’re going to be unveiling a new building, so it’s just the beginning of an exciting and much larger project.” After brief introductions from Brian Pascus, a finance reporter at Commercial Observer, and Jennifer Brown, senior vice president for the Sunset Park district at NYCEDC, attendees were treated to a brief discussion for a deeper introduction to MADE Bush Terminal. Waverly Neer, vice president of asset management at NYCEDC, held a short panel discussion with two of the building’s earliest tenants: lighting manufacturer Stickbulb and design studio Pelle. Pelle, run by husband and wife architect team Jean and Oliver Pelle, moved into Building
bullets: MADE Bush Terminal (Brooklyn mixed-use) hosts light industrial, brewery, 1K-capacity music venue.
NYCEDC handles leasing; Building A operational with tenants; industrial-mixed-use conversion.
Event-driven activation; Brooklyn industrial market demonstration project.
stories.summary: Commercial Observer hosted a summer networking event at MADE Bush Terminal, a new mixed-use complex in Sunset Park, Brooklyn. The development, anchored by light industrial tenants, includes a brewery, 1,000-capacity music venue, and other flex-industrial space. NYCEDC's Bridget Chansakul manages leasing for the project, which is actively attracting both industrial and creative tenants to Building A.
stories.impact: MADE Bush Terminal represents successful industrial-mixed-use conversion in Brooklyn; market appetite for flex industrial + ground-floor activation (food/beverage/entertainment) is strong. However, story is primarily about industrial/mixed-use, not multifamily; limited direct residential demand signal, though ground-floor activation + industrial tenancy may support adjacent or upper-floor residential if densified.

stories.title: Logistics Firm Highline Commerce Grows to 60K SF at Industry City, Thanks to Robots
stories.excerpt: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce is expanding its industrial footprint at Brooklyn’s Industry City for the second time in four years, Commercial Observer has learned. Highline’s lease renewal at 67 34th Street in Brooklyn’s Sunset Park neighborhood tacked on another 18,000 square feet to its warehouse space. The five-year deal rounds out the company’s total footprint at the mixed-use commercial campus to 60,000 square feet. The Brooklyn-based logistics company specializes in fulfilling orders for early-stage e-commerce brands, according to its website. Highline credits its growth, in part, to neighborly robots. The company has teamed up with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to install “humanoid robots” that are fulfilling up to 30 percent of Highline’s clients’ orders, according to an Industry City spokesperson. The 60,000-square-foot flexible industrial space will remain largely dedicated to fulfillment operations, along with some office space and social rooms. Highline Commerce negotiated the deal directly with Industry City’s Jeff Fein and Brad Blum, who represent the landlord in-house. Asking rent was $28 per square foot. Highline arrived at Industry City from Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood in 2022. During its initial four-year lease, the company’s warehouse expanded from 27,000 square feet to 42,000 square feet. Highline plans to remain at Industry City as long as it can keep growing. “New York is in the middle of an e-commerce golden age, and brands are launching faster than the logistics world can support them,” Richard Hurley, founder and CEO of Highline, said in a statement. “Serving them doesn’t just take a village, it takes a place like Industry City, where on-site manufacturing, printing, fulfillment and the robots that run it all operate within feet of one another.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Highline Commerce renews at Industry City; adds 18K SF to 60K SF total (Sunset Park, Brooklyn).
E-commerce fulfillment tenant; partnered with Ultra Robotics for humanoid robots (30% order fulfillment).
Logistics tenant growth signals e-commerce demand; second expansion in four years.
stories.summary: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce expanded its footprint at Brooklyn's Industry City to 60,000 square feet via a five-year renewal adding 18,000 square feet to its existing warehouse space in Sunset Park. The company specializes in early-stage e-commerce fulfillment and has deployed humanoid robots (via partner Ultra Robotics) to handle up to 30% of client orders. The space includes fulfillment operations, office, and social areas; this is Highline's second major expansion at Industry City in four years.
stories.impact: E-commerce logistics tenant demand remains strong in Brooklyn industrial; robot adoption signals labor-cost mitigation and operational efficiency scaling. Industrial market resilience in Sunset Park supports mixed-use asset sustainability, but direct multifamily impact is limited unless residential density is added above or adjacent to industrial; however, job and population retention in logistics-heavy Brooklyn submarket may underpin residential demand indirectly.

stories.title: Bakery Shop Cafe Aroma to Open New Spot on the Upper East Side
stories.excerpt: Time to rise and grind, Upper East Siders, as a new coffee shop and bakery is set to open at Avenues Equity Partners’ 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill. Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma has taken 2,200 square feet of retail space at the base of the five-story residential building, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for retail space on the Upper East Side was $252 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. Meridian’s Jacob Mayer represented the landlord in this deal, while Shai Baradarian and Tamir Baradarian of eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma. The Baradarians did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “1410 Madison Avenue is a rare turnkey restaurant space in a high-traffic Upper East Side location, and it generated strong interest from operators looking to be near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill’s dense residential base,” Mayer said in a statement. “Cafe Aroma is a great fit for the space, and we’re pleased to have completed a lease that positions both the landlord and the tenant for long-term success in the corridor.” This will be Cafe Aroma’s sixth Manhattan location and its first on the Upper East Side. Avenues Equity Partners acquired 1410 Madison Avenue and 1412 Madison Avenue for $6.3 million in January 2025, according to property records. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Cafe Aroma (family-owned coffee/bakery) leased 2.2K SF on Upper East Side; 1410 Madison Avenue.
Ground-floor retail in 5-story residential building; location near Mount Sinai, Carnegie Hill.
UES retail asking rents $252/SF (Q2 2026); high-traffic residential location.
stories.summary: Family-owned Cafe Aroma secured 2,200 square feet of ground-floor retail space at 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill (Upper East Side, Manhattan) in a transaction facilitated by Meridian Capital Group. The location benefits from proximity to Mount Sinai Hospital and Carnegie Hill's dense residential base. Q2 2026 UES retail asking rents averaged $252 per square foot; the property's turnkey condition and high-traffic location generated strong operator interest.
stories.impact: Ground-floor retail leasing activity and strong operator demand in Carnegie Hill signal healthy residential density and foot traffic; retail rents at $252/SF indicate robust consumer activity supporting residential values. However, story is primarily retail-centric; minor direct signal, though ground-floor activation supports multifamily asset value in luxury residential markets.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Musk-affiliated companies occupy 35K SF at Seaholm Power Plant (Downtown Austin); $5.2M renovation underway.
Wit Tech (SpaceX-linked) managing renovations; Terafab semiconductor plant planned in Grimes County.
Tesla presence in Austin area; Musk portfolio expanding; $55B Terafab project announced.
stories.summary: Multiple Elon Musk-affiliated companies are undergoing a $5.2 million office renovation at the Seaholm Power Plant (800 West Cesar Chavez Street) in Downtown Austin, where they occupy 35,000 square feet. Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, is managing the project. This activity is tied to Musk's $55 billion Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant planned in Grimes County, which the company has hinted could eventually reach 100 million square feet. Musk has also tripled land holdings in Bastrop (east of Austin) for data-center projects.
stories.impact: Massive Musk ecosystem expansion in Austin area (Terafab, data centers, land acquisitions) signals major employer growth and capital inflow into Austin metro; expected significant job creation and population in-migration. This is a multi-year, multi-billion-dollar employment anchor; multifamily developers should prioritize land acquisition in Grimes County, Bastrop, and greater Austin metros now, ahead of construction ramping (likely 2027–2028+). Terafab alone could drive 10,000+ high-wage jobs and supporting population; residential demand will be acute and cap rates likely to compress as capital flows in.

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: HueHub: 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers in West Little River, Miami; $55B+ project planned.
Live Local Act project; 100% furnished, attainably priced; exceeds 40% affordable requirement.
Spanish developer Pablo Castro faces lawsuits; project secured approvals but facing legal/reputational headwinds.
stories.summary: Spanish multifamily developer Pablo Castro is advancing HueHub, Florida's largest known multifamily complex planned for West Little River (Miami), comprising 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres. Developed under Florida's Live Local Act, the entire project will be furnished and attainably priced, exceeding the 40% affordable-unit requirement. Despite project approvals, Castro faces lawsuits from three local industry players alleging non-payment for early groundwork; the developer's lack of U.S. track record and litigation create execution risk and reputational uncertainty.
stories.impact: HueHub's scale (4,040 units) and Live Local Act structure (100% attainable pricing, furnished) represent bold institutional-grade supply addition to Miami multifamily market. However, developer's litigation risk and anonymity in U.S. market raises execution and financing stability concerns. If project advances, it will meaningfully increase Miami supply (potentially 500+ units/year 2027–2032), which could soften rent growth and cap-rate compression in West Little River and surrounding neighborhoods; Miami developers should monitor HueHub's funding and construction pace closely, as delivery timeline will determine competitive intensity.

stories.title: Faena Residences developers presell $30M penthouse for record price along Miami River
stories.excerpt: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna’s joint venture presold a penthouse at their Faena-branded luxury condo development along the Miami River for $30 million, a record for the area on price as well as price per square foot.  Karmely’s KAR Properties has long planned to develop the waterfront site into the Rafael Viñoly-designed project. In 2022, Karmely brought on Defortuna’s Fortune International Group as a partner. Last year, they launched the Faena Residences.  A mystery buyer is under contract to buy a penthouse in the east tower that will span 8,200 square feet of interior space and also include 7,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Faena Residences (Miami River): $30M penthouse presale; $3,700/SF; mystery buyer under contract.
Rafael Viñoly-designed luxury condo; east tower duplex; 8.2K SF interior + 7K SF outdoor.
Record price and PSF for Miami River; presales before groundbreaking signal strong high-end demand.
stories.summary: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna's Faena Residences joint venture presold a penthouse unit for $30 million ($3,700 per square foot), setting a neighborhood record for the Miami River area. The duplex penthouse spans 8,200 square feet of interior plus 7,000 square feet of outdoor space with private rooftop, pool, and wellness areas. The Rafael Viñoly-designed luxury condo project, launched in 2025, is generating high-price presales months before groundbreaking.
stories.impact: Ultra-luxury Miami River presales at $3,700/SF signal strong international capital and high-net-worth demand for waterfront trophy assets; presale activity (before completion, similar to Mandarin Residences and Auberge Shore Club) indicates liquidity and buyer confidence in Miami luxury market. While not mass-market multifamily, this signals sustained capital inflow and demonstrates feasibility of high-price-point development financing; developers targeting Miami waterfront luxury can point to Faena's presale traction. However, this is niche ultra-luxury; broader market implications limited to capital-availability signals.

stories.title: LA city’s top construction permits: Week ending July 11, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 The largest development to receive permits last week was for a single-family build in Encino, where plans were filed for 11,278 square feet of work across two permits at 3864 North Kim Lane. The project includes a new two-story single-family dwelling with an attached three-car garage and a pond. Job Filing Numbers: 25010-10000-05697, 25010-10000-05874, 25047-10000-01751 and more 📊 In Valley Glen, permits were issued for 8,765 square feet of construction across three permits at 14414-14418 1/2 West Tiara Street. The project includes building a new accessory dwelling unit, along with a three-story duplex. The work is estimated to cost […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LA construction permits (week ending 7/11): Encino single-family (11.3K SF), Valley Glen ADU/duplex (8.8K SF).
Bel Air megamansion "The One" (Richard Saghian, Fashion Nova owner): 75.9K SF alteration; $350K+ cost.
Single-family and ADU permitting activity; high-value alterations in elite neighborhoods.
stories.summary: LA's top construction permits for the week ending July 11, 2026 included: a 11,278-square-foot two-story single-family home in Encino; an 8,765-square-foot ADU and three-story duplex in Valley Glen (est. $880K); and a 75,856-square-foot alteration to "The One" megamansion in Bel Air (est. $350K+, owned by Fashion Nova's Richard Saghian). Permitting activity reflects both new ground-up residential and significant alterations in elite neighborhoods.
stories.impact: LA permitting data shows active single-family and ADU activity (potential supply constraint relief) but limited multifamily groundwork signals in this sample. ADU and duplex permitting suggests zoning flexibility and secondary-unit demand in middle-tier LA neighborhoods (Valley Glen); developers should monitor ADU-friendly zoning expansion as a proxy for affordability/density policy shifts. Megamansion alterations ("The One") signal strong ultra-luxury market, but no mass-market multifamily signal in this week's top permits.

stories.title: Jed Walentas, Tom Brady’s ex-manager abruptly settle legal battle over Bahamas mansion
stories.excerpt: A game of catch-me-if-you-can between Jed Walentas and Tom Brady’s former manager over a hurricane-damaged Bahamas mansion has come to an abrupt end. The Two Trees Management CEO had been trying to collect a $300,000 arbitration award from Will McDonough since last July, but repeatedly failed to locate him for formal legal service, court filings show. On Monday, Walentas notched a win when Manhattan Supreme Court Justice Kathleen Waterman-Marshall authorized Walentas to serve McDonough via email and set October 27 as the date McDonough or his legal team must show up at court to explain why he shouldn’t be forced […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Jed Walentas (Two Trees Management) vs. Will McDonough (Tom Brady ex-manager) settled; Bahamas mansion dispute.
Bunkers Investments, LLC (2015): $4.9M combined capital; hurricane damage; $300K arbitration award dispute.
Settlement after suit nearly served; unclear terms; prior relationship damage.
stories.summary: A legal dispute between Two Trees Management CEO Jed Walentas and Tom Brady's former manager Will McDonough over a luxury rental property in Bakers Bay (Bahamas) was abruptly settled on October 27, 2024. Walentas had sought to collect a $300,000 arbitration award; the partners formed Bunkers Investments in 2015 and contributed a combined $4.9 million to acquire and build the property before hurricane damage required extensive repairs. McDonough told The Real Deal the dispute had been settled months prior, and the matter was withdrawn with no payment disclosed.
stories.impact: Story has minimal relevance to multifamily development; primarily a high-net-worth real estate partnership/litigation matter. No market signals on capital availability, construction costs, or residential demand. The Bahamas luxury property development context is geographically irrelevant to U.S. multifamily markets.

stories.title: Fort Worth Stockyards will get $71M apartment project as $1B planned redevelopment hangs in balance
stories.excerpt: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71 million, 296-unit affordable apartment complex north of Fort Worth’s historic Stockyards. The apartments are a small slice of a 14.6-acre mixed-use development from Oldham Goodwin aimed at enhancing the Stockyards. The apartment complex features affordable units in partnership with Fort Worth Housing Solutions, and is slated to be the residential portion of the wider development. Amenities include a pool, clubhouse and a dog park. The apartment sizes begin at studio sizes and scale to three-bedroom units, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  Stockyards North, which […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Worth: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange; 296 units, $71M.
Affordable housing; studios to 3-bed units; Stockyards North mixed-use district (14.6 acres).
Stockyards 2nd phase ($1B renovation) mired in legal dispute; first phase successful.
stories.summary: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71 million, 296-unit affordable apartment complex in the Stockyards North development (14.6 acres north of historic Stockyards). The project includes studio to three-bedroom units, pool, clubhouse, and dog park. The development is anchored by the apartment complex and planned to include a Home2Suites hotel, retail, and dining in a walkable, affordable mixed-use district. The wider Stockyards 2nd phase ($1 billion renovation) is mired in legal disputes, while the first phase successfully balanced heritage preservation and modern amenities.
stories.impact: The Exchange represents Fort Worth Housing Solutions' first Stockyards-area development and signals institutional affordable housing appetite in a historic tourism district. The $71M/$240K per-unit basis for 296 affordable units suggests stable acquisition and construction financing for workforce housing in Fort Worth. However, the $1B second-phase Stockyards renovation legal dispute creates uncertainty and may delay mixed-use catalysts. Developers should monitor Stockyards dispute resolution; a positive outcome could accelerate broader redevelopment and increase multifamily demand in the Stockyards submarket.
